questions.option_a: The ceiling laws were aimed at family holdings and not individual holdings.
questions.option_b: The major aim of land reforms was providing agricultural land to all the landless.
questions.option_c: It resulted in cultivation of cash crops as a predominant form of cultivation.
questions.option_d: Land reforms permitted no exemptions to the ceiling limits.
questions.explanation: Answer B. The correct statement is that a major aim of land reforms was to provide agricultural land to the landless, along with tenancy security and abolition of intermediaries. Ceiling laws applied to individual and family holdings with several exemptions, and the reforms did not make cash crops the predominant form of cultivation, so the other options are wrong.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: The Global Competitiveness Report is published by the
questions.option_b: United Nations Conference on Trade and Development
questions.explanation: Answer C. The Global Competitiveness Report, which ranks countries on the drivers of productivity and competitiveness, is published by the World Economic Forum. It is not brought out by the IMF, UNCTAD or the World Bank.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
Consider the following statements about ‘the Charter Act of 1813’:
1. It ended the trade monopoly of the East India Company in India except for trade in tea and trade with China.
2. It asserted the sovereignty of the British Crown over the Indian territories held by the Company.
3. The revenues of India were now controlled by the British Parliament.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer A. The Charter Act of 1813 ended the East India Company's trade monopoly in India, retaining its monopoly only over the tea trade and trade with China, and it asserted the sovereignty of the British Crown over the Company's Indian territories, so statements 1 and 2 are correct. It did not place Indian revenues under British parliamentary control, so statement 3 is wrong.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
With reference to Swadeshi Movement consider the following statements:
1. It contributed to the revival of the indigenous artisan crafts and industries.
2. The National Council of Education was established as a part of Swadeshi Movement.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer C. The Swadeshi Movement encouraged a revival of indigenous artisan crafts and industries as part of the boycott of foreign goods, and the National Council of Education was set up during this period to promote national education. Both statements are therefore correct.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Movement/Organization Leader
1. All India Anti- Untouchability League : Mahatma Gandhi
2. All India Kisan Sabha : Swami Sahajanand Saraswati
3. Self-Respect Movement : E. V. Ramaswami Naicker Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer D. All three pairs are correctly matched: Mahatma Gandhi founded the All India Anti-Untouchability League (later Harijan Sevak Sangh), Swami Sahajanand Saraswati led the All India Kisan Sabha, and E. V. Ramaswami Naicker (Periyar) led the Self-Respect Movement in the south.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: Which one of the following is not a Harappan site?
questions.explanation: Answer C. Sohgaura, known for an early inscription in the Ganga plains, is not a Harappan site. Chanhudaro, Kot Diji and Desalpur are all associated with the Indus Valley Civilization, making Sohgaura the exception.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: In which of the following relief sculpture inscriptions is ‘Ranyo Ashoka’ (King Ashoka) mentioned along with the stone portrait of Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Answer A. The relief sculpture at Kanganahalli, a Buddhist site in Karnataka, carries the label 'Ranyo Ashoka' identifying a stone portrait of the emperor Ashoka. This depiction with the inscribed name is not associated with Sanchi, Shahbazgarhi or Sohgaura.

questions.subject: Art & Culture
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Deification of the Buddha
2. Trading the path of Bodhisattvas
3. Image worship and rituals
Which of the above is/are the feature/features of Mahayana Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Answer D. All three are features of Mahayana Buddhism: the Buddha came to be worshipped as a divine figure, the ideal of the Bodhisattva who postpones nirvana to help others was central, and image worship along with elaborate rituals developed. Hence all statements apply.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: With refence to forced labour (Vishti) in India during the Gupta period, which one of the following statements is correct?
questions.option_a: It was considered a source of income for the State, a sort of tax paid by the people.
questions.option_b: It was totally absent in the Madhya Pradesh and Kathiawar region of the Gupta Empire.
questions.option_c: The forced labourer was entitled to weekly wages.
questions.option_d: The eldest son of the labourer was sent as the forced labourer.
questions.explanation: Answer A. During the Gupta period, forced labour (vishti) was treated as a form of tax or source of revenue for the State, with people obliged to render unpaid labour. It was not confined to particular regions, the labourer was not paid weekly wages, and there was no rule of sending the eldest son, so only option A is correct.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: Which one of the following groups of plants was domesticated in the ‘New World’ and introduced into the ‘Old World’?
questions.explanation: Answer A. Tobacco, cocoa and rubber were all domesticated in the Americas (the New World) and later introduced to the Old World after the Columbian exchange. The other groups include crops like cotton, coffee, sugarcane and wheat that were Old World domesticates, so they do not fit.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Asiatic lion is naturally found in India only.
2. Double-humped camel is naturally found in India only.
3. One-horned rhinoceros is naturally found in India only.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer A. Only statement 1 is correct: the Asiatic lion in the wild is naturally confined to India, in the Gir region. The double-humped (Bactrian) camel also occurs naturally in Central Asia, and the one-horned rhinoceros is found in Nepal as well, so statements 2 and 3 are wrong.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Famous Place River
1. Pandharpur : Chandrabhaga
2. Tiruchirappalli : Cauvery
3. Hampi : Malaprabha Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer A. Pandharpur lies on the Chandrabhaga (Bhima) river and Tiruchirappalli is on the Cauvery, so pairs 1 and 2 are correctly matched. Hampi stands on the Tungabhadra, not the Malaprabha, so pair 3 is wrongly matched and is excluded.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: In a given year in India, official poverty lines are higher in some States than in others because
questions.option_a: poverty rates vary from State to State
questions.option_b: price levels vary from State to State
questions.option_c: Gross State Product varies from State to State
questions.option_d: quality of public distribution varies from State to State
questions.explanation: Answer B. Official poverty lines differ across States mainly because price levels, and therefore the cost of the minimum consumption basket, vary from State to State. Differences in poverty rates, gross state product, or the quality of the PDS do not by themselves determine the poverty line, so option B is correct.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: In the context of which of the following do some scientists suggest the used of cirrus cloud thinning technique and the injection of sulphate aerosol into stratosphere?
questions.option_a: Creating the artificial rains in some regions
questions.option_b: Reducing the frequency and intensity of tropical cyclones
questions.option_c: Reducing the adverse effects of solar wind on the Earth
questions.explanation: Answer D. Cirrus cloud thinning and injecting sulphate aerosols into the stratosphere are proposed geoengineering or climate-intervention techniques aimed at reducing global warming by allowing more heat to escape or reflecting incoming sunlight. They are not primarily about artificial rain, cyclones, or solar wind.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: In the context of which one of the following are the terms ‘pyrolysis and plasma gasification’ mentioned?
questions.explanation: Answer D. Pyrolysis and plasma gasification are thermal processes used in waste-to-energy technologies, breaking down solid or municipal waste at high temperatures to recover energy or produce syngas. They are not techniques for extracting rare earths, natural gas, or running hydrogen fuel vehicles.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: Which of the following are in Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve?
questions.option_a: Neyyar, Peppara and Shendurney Wildlife Sanctuaries; and Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve
questions.option_b: Mudumalai, Sathyamangalam and Wayanand Wildlife Sanctuaries; and Silent Valley National Park
questions.option_c: Kaundinya, Gundla Brahmeswaram and Papikonda Wildlife Sanctuaries; and Mukurthi National Park
questions.option_d: Kawal and Sri Venkateswara Wildlife Sanctuaries; and Nagarjunasagar-Srisailam Tiger Reserve
questions.explanation: Answer A. The Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve, spanning the southern Western Ghats in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, includes the Neyyar, Peppara and Shendurney Wildlife Sanctuaries and the Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve. The other options list protected areas belonging to different biosphere reserves and regions.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Some species of turtles are herbivores.
2. Some species of fish are herbivores.
3. Some species of marine mammals are herbivores.
4. Some species of snakes are viviparous.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer D. All four statements are correct: some turtles are herbivores, many fish species are herbivorous, marine mammals like the dugong and manatee are plant-eaters, and several snakes, such as vipers and sea snakes, are viviparous, giving birth to live young rather than laying eggs.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Wildlife Naturally found in
1. Blue-finned Mahseer : Cauvery River
2. Irrawaddy Dolphin : Chambal River
3. Rusty-spotted Cat : Eastern Ghats Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer C. The Blue-finned Mahseer is naturally found in the Cauvery river system and the Rusty-spotted Cat occurs in the Eastern Ghats, so pairs 1 and 3 are correct. The Irrawaddy Dolphin is associated with brackish coastal waters and Chilika Lake, not the Chambal, so pair 2 is wrong.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: Why is there a great concern about the ‘microbeads’ that are released into environment?
questions.option_a: They are considered harmful to marine ecosystems.
questions.option_b: They are considered to cause skin cancer in children.
questions.option_c: They are small enough to be absorbed by crop plants in irrigated fields.
questions.option_d: They are often found to be used as food adulterants.
questions.explanation: Answer A. Microbeads are tiny plastic particles used in some cosmetics and personal-care products that pass through water treatment and enter waterways, where they harm marine life and accumulate in the food chain. The concern is their damage to marine ecosystems rather than skin cancer, uptake by crops, or use as food adulterants.

questions.subject: Art & Culture
questions.text: Building ‘Kalyaana Mandapas’ was a notable feature in the temple construction in the kingdom of
questions.explanation: Answer D. The construction of ornate Kalyana Mandapas, or marriage halls, within temple complexes was a distinctive feature of Vijayanagara temple architecture, alongside grand pillared halls and towering gopurams. This feature is specifically linked to the Vijayanagara kingdom rather than the Chalukyas, Chandelas or Rashtrakutas.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. In the revenue administration of Delhi Sultanate, the in-charge of revenue collection was known as ‘Amil’.
2. The Iqta system of Sultans of Delhi was an ancient indigenous institution.
3. The office of ‘Mir Bakshi’ came into existence during the reign of Khalji Sultans of Delhi.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer A. Only statement 1 is correct: in the Delhi Sultanate's revenue administration the official in charge of revenue collection was called the Amil. The Iqta was an institution introduced by the Turks and not an ancient indigenous one, and the office of Mir Bakshi is associated with the Mughals, not the Khaljis, so statements 2 and 3 are wrong.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Saint Nimbarka was a contemporary of Akbar.
2. Saint Kabir was greatly influenced by Shaikh Ahmad Sirhindi.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer D. Neither statement is correct. Nimbarka, the Vaishnava saint-philosopher, lived well before Akbar's time rather than being his contemporary, and Kabir predated Shaikh Ahmad Sirhindi, so he could not have been influenced by him. Both pairings are therefore chronologically wrong.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
With reference to the British colonial rule in India, consider the following statements:
1. Mahatma Gandhi was instrumental in the abolition of the system of ‘indentured labour’.
2. In Lord Chelmsford’s War Conference’, Mahatma Gandhi did not support the resolution on recruiting Indians for World War.
3. Consequent upon the breaking of Salt Law by Indian people, the Indian National Congress was declared illegal by the colonial rulers.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer B. Mahatma Gandhi was instrumental in ending the indentured labour system, and following the breaking of the Salt Law during the civil disobedience movement the colonial government declared the Congress illegal, so statements 1 and 3 are correct. At Lord Chelmsford's War Conference Gandhi actually supported the recruitment resolution, so statement 2 is wrong.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
With reference to Indian National Movement, consider the following pairs: Person Position held
1. Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru : President, All India Liberal Federation
2. K. C. Neogy : Member, The Constituent Assembly
3. P. C. Joshi : General Secretary, Communist Party of India Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer D. All three pairs match correctly: Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru was associated with the presidency of the All India Liberal Federation, K. C. Neogy was a member of the Constituent Assembly, and P. C. Joshi served as General Secretary of the Communist Party of India.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: With reference to Mian Tansen, which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_a: Tansen was the title given to him by Emperor Akbar.
questions.option_b: Tansen composed Dhrupada on Hindu gods and goddesses.
questions.option_c: Tansen composed songs on his patrons.
questions.explanation: Answer A. The incorrect statement is that 'Tansen' was a title conferred by Akbar. Tansen was one of Akbar's celebrated court musicians who composed dhrupads on Hindu deities and on his patrons and is credited with several ragas, but the name Tansen was not a title given to him by the emperor in the manner described.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: Who among the following Mughal Emperors shifted emphasis from illustrated manuscripts to album and individual portrait?
questions.explanation: Answer C. It was under Jahangir that Mughal painting shifted its emphasis from illustrated manuscripts towards individual portraits and album paintings, reflecting his personal interest in naturalistic studies and portraiture. This transition is credited to Jahangir rather than Humayun, Akbar or Shah Jahan.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: Which one of the following National Parks lies completely in the temperate alpine
questions.explanation: Answer D. The Valley of Flowers National Park in Uttarakhand lies entirely in the high-altitude temperate-alpine zone of the Himalayas, famed for its alpine meadows and flowers. Manas, Namdapha and Neora Valley extend into lower tropical or subtropical zones, so they are not wholly alpine.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: Atal Innovation Mission is set up under the
questions.option_d: Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship
questions.explanation: Answer C. The Atal Innovation Mission, which promotes innovation and entrepreneurship through initiatives like Atal Tinkering Labs and Atal Incubation Centres, is set up under NITI Aayog. It is not housed under the Department of Science and Technology or the ministries of labour or skill development.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text: On 21st June, the Sun
questions.option_a: does not set below the horizon at the Arctic Circle
questions.option_b: does not set below the horizon at Antarctic Circle
questions.option_c: shines vertically overhead at noon on the Equator
questions.option_d: shines vertically overhead at the Tropic of Capricorn
questions.explanation: Answer A. On 21 June, the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, the Sun is overhead at the Tropic of Cancer, and within the Arctic Circle it does not set below the horizon, producing continuous daylight. It does not stay above the horizon at the Antarctic Circle nor shine overhead at the Equator or the Tropic of Capricorn on that date.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Agricultural soils release nitrogen oxides into environment.
2. Cattle release ammonia into environment.
3. Poultry industry releases reactive nitrogen compounds into environment.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer D. All three statements are correct: agricultural soils, especially when fertilised, release nitrogen oxides, cattle emit ammonia through their waste, and the poultry industry releases reactive nitrogen compounds. Each is a recognised source of reactive nitrogen entering the environment.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text: What is common to the places known as Aliyar, Isapur and Kangsabati?
questions.explanation: Answer D. Aliyar, Isapur and Kangsabati are all names of water reservoirs or dams in India, associated with irrigation and water storage. They are not linked to uranium deposits, tropical rainforests or cave systems, so the common feature is that they are water reservoirs.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
In the context of proposals to the use of hydrogen- enriched CNG (H-CNG) as fuel for buses in public transport, consider the following statements:
1. The main advantage of the use of H-CNG is the elimination of carbon monoxide emissions.
2. H-CNG as fuel reduces carbon dioxide and hydrocarbon emissions.
3. Hydrogen up to one-fifth by volume can be blended with CNG as fuel for buses.
4. H-CNG makes the fuel less expensive than CNG.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer B. Blending hydrogen with CNG (H-CNG) reduces carbon dioxide and hydrocarbon emissions, and up to about one-fifth by volume of hydrogen can be blended into CNG, supporting statements 2 and 3. It reduces but does not eliminate carbon monoxide, and it is not cheaper than plain CNG, so statements 1 and 4 are wrong.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text: Why are dewdrops not formed on a cloudy night?
questions.option_a: Clouds absorb the radiation released from the Earth’s surface.
questions.option_b: Clouds reflect back the Earth’s radiation.
questions.option_c: The Earth’s surface would have low temperature on cloudy nights.
questions.option_d: Clouds deflect the blowing wind to ground level.
questions.explanation: Answer B. Dew forms when the ground cools sufficiently by radiating heat to a clear sky. On a cloudy night the clouds reflect the Earth's outgoing radiation back to the surface, keeping it warmer, so the ground does not cool enough for dew to condense. The key mechanism is the clouds reflecting back the Earth's radiation.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The 44th Amendment to the Constitution of India introduced an Article placing the election of the Prime Minister beyond judicial review.
2. The Supreme Court of India struck down the 99th Amendment to the Constitution of India as being violative of the independence of judiciary.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer B. The Supreme Court did strike down the 99th Amendment, which had created the National Judicial Appointments Commission, as violating the independence of the judiciary, so statement 2 is correct. Statement 1 is wrong: it was the 39th Amendment, not the 44th, that had tried to place the Prime Minister's election beyond judicial review.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The motion to impeach a Judge of the Supreme Court of Indian cannot be rejected by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha as per the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.
2. The Constitution of India defines and gives details of what constitutes ‘incapacity and proved misbehaviour’ of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India.
3. The details of the process of impeachment of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India are given in the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.
4. If the motion for the impeachment of a Judge is taken up for voting, the law requires the motion to be backed by each House of the Parliament and supported by a majority of total membership of that House and by not less than two- thirds of total members of that House present and voting
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer C. Statements 3 and 4 are correct: the process of removing a Supreme Court judge is detailed in the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968, and the motion must be passed by each House with a special majority of the House's total membership and at least two-thirds of members present and voting. The Speaker can reject an admission motion, and the Constitution does not itself define incapacity and misbehaviour, so statements 1 and 2 are wrong.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: The Ninth Schedule was introduced in the Constitution of India during the prime ministership of
questions.explanation: Answer A. The Ninth Schedule was added through the First Constitutional Amendment in 1951, during Jawaharlal Nehru's prime ministership, mainly to shield land-reform laws from judicial challenge. It therefore dates to Nehru's tenure rather than that of Shastri, Indira Gandhi or Morarji Desai.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Coal sector was nationalized by the Government of India under Indira Gandhi.
2. Now, coal blocks are allocated on lottery basis.
3. Till recently, India imported coal to meet the shortages of domestic supply, but now India is self- sufficient in coal product.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer A. Only statement 1 is correct: the coal sector was nationalised during Indira Gandhi's government in the 1970s. Coal blocks are not allocated by a lottery method, and India still imports large quantities of coal rather than being self-sufficient, so statements 2 and 3 are wrong.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Parliament (Prevention of Disqualification) Act, 1959 exempts several posts from disqualification on the grounds of ‘Office of Profit’.
2. The above-mentioned Act was amended five times.
3. The term ‘Office of Profit’ is well-defined in the Constitution of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer A. The Parliament (Prevention of Disqualification) Act, 1959 does exempt various offices from disqualification on grounds of holding an office of profit, and this Act has been amended several times, supporting statements 1 and 2. The term 'office of profit' is not clearly defined in the Constitution, so statement 3 is incorrect.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: Under which Schedule of the Constitution of India can the transfer of tribal land to private parties for mining be declared null and void?
questions.explanation: Answer B. It is under the Fifth Schedule, which provides for the administration of Scheduled Areas and the protection of tribal interests, that the transfer of tribal land to private parties for mining can be declared null and void. The Third, Ninth and Twelfth Schedules deal with unrelated matters like oaths, protected laws and urban local bodies.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: Recently, there was a growing awareness in our country about the importance of Himalayan nettle (Girardinia diversifolia) because it is found to be a sustainable source of
questions.explanation: Answer D. The Himalayan nettle (Girardinia diversifolia) has drawn attention as a sustainable source of natural textile fibre, which is extracted from the plant's stem and woven into fabric by hill communities. It is not valued as a source of anti-malarial drugs, biodiesel or paper pulp in this context.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.text:
For the measurement/ estimation of which of the following are satellite images/remote sensing data used?
1. Chlorophyll content in the vegetation of a specific location
2. Greenhouse gas emissions from rice paddies of a specific location
3. Land surface temperatures of a specific location
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanation: Answer D. Satellite imagery and remote sensing can estimate all three: chlorophyll content of vegetation through spectral reflectance indices, greenhouse-gas emissions such as methane from rice paddies, and land surface temperatures using thermal bands. Hence all three statements are correct.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following states:
1. Chhattisgarh
2. Madhya Pradesh
3. Maharashtra
4. Odisha With reference to the States mentioned above, in terms of percentage of forest cover to the total area of State, which one of the following is the correct ascending order?
questions.explanation: Answer C. Arranged by percentage of forest cover to total geographical area in ascending order, the sequence is Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha and then Chhattisgarh, which corresponds to 3-2-4-1. Chhattisgarh has the highest forest-cover proportion among these States and Maharashtra the lowest.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct about the deposits of ‘methane hydrate?
1. Global warming might trigger the release of methane gas from these deposits.
2. Large deposits of ‘methane hydrate’ are found in Arctic Tundra and under the seafloor.
3. Methane in atmosphere oxidizes to carbon dioxide after decade or two.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanation: Answer D. All three statements are correct: warming can destabilise methane hydrate deposits and release methane, large deposits occur in the Arctic tundra and beneath the seafloor, and atmospheric methane is oxidised to carbon dioxide over roughly a decade or two. Hence the full option is right.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following:
1. Carbon monoxide
2. Methane
3. Ozone
4. Sulphur dioxide
Which of the above are released into atmosphere due to the burning of crop/biomass residue?
questions.explanation: Answer D. Burning of crop and biomass residue is incomplete combustion that releases all the listed gases: carbon monoxide, methane, ozone precursors leading to ozone, and sulphur dioxide, along with particulates. Therefore all four are emitted into the atmosphere.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Sea Bordering country
1. Adriatic Sea : Albania
2. Black Sea : Croatia
3. Caspian Sea : Kazakhstan
4. Mediterranean Sea : Morocco
5. Red Sea : Syria Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer B. Albania borders the Adriatic Sea, Kazakhstan borders the Caspian Sea, and Morocco borders the Mediterranean Sea, so pairs 1, 3 and 4 are correct. Croatia does not border the Black Sea and Syria does not border the Red Sea, so pairs 2 and 5 are wrong.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: Among the following which one is the largest exporter of rice in the world in the last five years?
questions.explanation: Answer B. India has been the world's largest exporter of rice, shipping out large volumes of basmati and non-basmati rice to markets across Asia and Africa. It exports more than China, Myanmar or Vietnam, making India the correct answer.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Glacier River
1. Bandarpunch : Yamuna
2. Bara Shigri : Chenab
3. Milam : Mandakini
4. Siachen : Nubra
5. Zemu Manas Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Answer A. The Bandarpunch glacier feeds the Yamuna, the Bara Shigri glacier lies in the Chenab basin, and the Siachen glacier gives rise to the Nubra river, so pairs 1, 2 and 4 are correct. The Milam glacier is linked to the Gori Ganga (not the Mandakini) and the Zemu glacier to the Teesta (not the Manas), so those pairs are wrong.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: In India, the use of carbofuran, methyl parathion, phorate and triazophos is viewed with apprehension. These chemicals are used as
questions.explanation: Answer A. Carbofuran, methyl parathion, phorate and triazophos are all chemical pesticides (insecticides) used in agriculture, and they are viewed with concern because of their high toxicity to humans and non-target species. They are not food preservatives, fruit-ripening agents or cosmetic moisturisers.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Under Ramsar Convention, it is mandatory on the part of the Government of India to protect and conserve all the wetlands in the territory of India.
2. The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2010 were framed by the Government of India based on the recommendation of Ramsar Convention.
3. The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2010 also encompass the drainage area or catchment regions of the wetlands as determined by the authority.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Answer B. The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2010 were framed drawing on the Ramsar Convention's approach and they extend to the catchment or drainage areas of wetlands as determined by the authority, so statements 2 and 3 are correct. The Ramsar Convention does not legally oblige India to protect every wetland in its territory, so statement 1 is wrong.

papers.source_note: Real questions from UPSC Civil Services Prelims 2018 (GS Paper I), a public government examination paper. Explanations are AIVORA-authored and original; not copied from any coaching material.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. In the first Lok Sabha, the single largest party in the opposition was the SwatantraParty
2. In the Lok Sabha, a "Leader of the Opposition" was recognised for the first time in 1969.
3. In the Lok Sabha, if a party does not have a minimum of 75 members, its leader cannot be recognised as the Leader of the Opposition.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Swatantra Party was founded only in 1959, so it could not have existed in the first Lok Sabha of 1952, making statement 1 wrong. The office of Leader of the Opposition first gained official recognition in 1969, so statement 2 is correct. Statement 3 is wrong because recognition requires strength equal to one-tenth of the House, about 55 members, not 75.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
Which of the following leaf modifications occur(s) in the desert areas to inhibit water loss?
1. Hard and waxy leaves
2. Tiny leaves
3. Thorns instead of leaves
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: In arid regions plants cut transpiration through several adaptations: thick, hard and waxy cuticles reduce evaporation, very small leaves lower the surface area exposed to the sun, and in extreme cases leaves are reduced to spines or thorns while the stem takes over photosynthesis. All three modifications help conserve water, so every statement is correct.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
As per the NSSO 70th Round "Situation Assessment Survey of Agricultural Households", consider the following statements:
1. Rajasthan has the highest percentage share of agricultural households among its rural households.
2. Out of the total agricultural households in the country, a little over 60 percent belong to OBCs.
3. In Kerala, a little over 60 percent of agricultural households reported to have received maximum income from source other than agricultural activities.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Rajasthan records a very high share of agricultural households among its rural households, and in Kerala most such households earn the bulk of their income from non-farm sources, so statements 1 and 3 hold. Statement 2 is wrong because the OBC share of agricultural households is closer to 45 percent, not above 60 percent.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
How is the National Green Tribunal (NGT) different from the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)?
1. The NGT has been established by an Act whereas the CPCB has been created by an executive order of the Government.
2. The NGT provides environmental justice and helps reduce the burden of litigation in the higher courts whereas the CPCB promotes cleanliness of streams and wells, and aims to improve the quality of air in the country.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect because the CPCB was set up under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, a statute, not a mere executive order. Statement 2 correctly captures their roles: the NGT delivers speedy environmental justice and eases court backlogs, while the CPCB works to keep water bodies clean and improve air quality.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Parliament of India can place a particular law in the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India.
2. The validity of a law placed in the Ninth Schedule cannot be examined by any court and no judgement can be made on it.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Under Article 31B, Parliament can indeed add a law to the Ninth Schedule to protect it, so statement 1 is correct. Statement 2 is wrong because the Supreme Court held in I.R. Coelho (2007) that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule after 24 April 1973 remain open to judicial review if they violate the Constitution's basic structure.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: Which one of the following best describes the term "Merchant Discount Rate" sometimes seen in news?
questions.option_a: The incentive given by a bank to a merchant for accepting payments through debit cards pertaining to that bank.
questions.option_b: The amount paid back by banks to their customers when they use debit cards for financial transactions for purchaing goods or services.
questions.option_c: The charge to a merchant by a bank for accepting payments from his customers through the bank's debit cards.
questions.option_d: The incentive given by the Government to merchants for promoting digital payments by their customers through Point of Sale (PoS) machines and debit cards.
questions.explanation: The Merchant Discount Rate is the fee a bank charges a merchant for processing payments received from customers through cards. It is a cost borne by the merchant, not an incentive or cashback, which rules out the other options that describe payments made to merchants or customers.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
What is/are the consequence/consequences of a country becoming the member of the 'Nuclear Suppliers Group'?
1. It will have access to the latest and most efficient nuclear technologies.
2. It automatically becomes a member of "The Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT)".
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Joining the Nuclear Suppliers Group eases a country's access to advanced nuclear technology and materials by relaxing export controls, so statement 1 is correct. Statement 2 is wrong because NSG membership is entirely separate from the NPT and does not confer automatic membership of that treaty.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to India's decision to levy an equalization tax of 6% on online advertisement services offered by non- resident entities, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. It is introduced as a part of the Income Tax Act.
2. Non-resident entities that offer advertisement services in India can claim a tax credit in their home country under the "Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements".
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: The equalisation levy was introduced through the Finance Act, 2016 as a distinct charge, not as part of the Income Tax Act, so statement 1 is wrong. Statement 2 is also wrong because, since the levy is not an income tax, foreign firms cannot claim credit for it under Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management (FRBM) Review Committee Report has recommended a debt to GDP ratio of 60% for the general (combined) government by 2023, comprising 40% for the Central Government and 20% for the State Governments.
2. The Central Government has domestic liabilities of 21% of GDP as compared to that of 49% of GDP of the State Governments.
3. As per the Constitution of India, it is mandatory for a State to take the Central Government's consent for raising any loan if the former owes any outstanding liabilities to the latter.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The FRBM Review Committee did recommend a combined debt-to-GDP ceiling of 60 percent, split 40 percent Centre and 20 percent States, so statement 1 is correct, and Article 293 requires a State to obtain the Centre's consent for fresh borrowing while it owes the Centre money, making statement 3 correct. Statement 2 reverses the figures, so it is wrong.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The quantity of imported edible oils is more than the domestic production of edible oils in the last five years.
2. The Government does not impose any customs duty on all the imported edible oils as a special case.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: India's demand for edible oils far outstrips its domestic output, so imports have exceeded home production in recent years, making statement 1 correct. Statement 2 is wrong because the government does levy customs duties on imported edible oils and adjusts these rates to manage prices and protect farmers.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: He wrote biographies of Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji and Shrikrishna; stayed in America for some time; and was also elected to the Central Assembly. He was
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai authored biographical works on figures such as Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji and Shri Krishna, spent time in the United States during the First World War, and later served in the Central Legislative Assembly. These details point to him rather than the other leaders listed.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Aadhaar card can be used as a proof of citizenship or domicile.
2. Once issued, Aadhaar number cannot be deactivated or omitted by the Issuing Authority.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Aadhaar number is only a proof of identity, not of citizenship or domicile, so statement 1 is wrong. Statement 2 is also incorrect because the issuing authority can deactivate or omit an Aadhaar number, for example on grounds of duplication or failure to meet requirements.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Which of the following has/have shrunk immensely/dried up the recent past due to human activities?
1. Aral Sea
2. Black Sea
3. Lake Baikal
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: The Aral Sea in Central Asia has shrunk dramatically, largely because the rivers feeding it were diverted for irrigation, so statement 1 is correct. The Black Sea and Lake Baikal, the world's deepest freshwater lake, have not dried up in this way, which eliminates the other options.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: "Rule of Law Index" is released by which of the following?
questions.option_c: The Office of UN Commissioner for Human Rights
questions.explanation: The Rule of Law Index is compiled and released by the World Justice Project, which the correct option corresponds to. Amnesty International, the International Court of Justice and the UN human rights office do not publish this particular index, so they are ruled out.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: Which one of the following links all the ATMs in India
questions.explanation: The National Payments Corporation of India operates the National Financial Switch, the network that interconnects the country's ATMs and enables interoperable transactions. The RBI is the regulator rather than the operator of this switch, so the other options do not fit.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) is the amount that banks have to maintain in the form of their own funds to offset any loss that banks incur if the account-holders fail to repay dues.
2. CAR is decided by each individual bank.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Capital Adequacy Ratio is correctly described as the cushion of a bank's own funds held to absorb losses if borrowers default, so statement 1 is right. Statement 2 is wrong because the ratio is not set by each bank individually; it is prescribed by the RBI in line with Basel norms.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
The identity platform 'Aadhaar' provides open "Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)". What does it imply?
1. It can be integrated into any electronic device.
2. Online authentication using iris is possible.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Open Application Programming Interfaces let external developers integrate Aadhaar authentication into any electronic device or application, so statement 1 is correct. The platform also supports biometric authentication including iris scans, making statement 2 correct as well, so both statements hold.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: Very recently, in which of the following countries have lakhs of people either suffered from severe famine/acute malnutrition or died due to starvation caused by war/ethnic conflicts?
questions.explanation: Yemen and South Sudan have both seen large populations pushed into severe famine, acute malnutrition and starvation driven by prolonged war and internal conflict. The other pairs of countries listed were not associated with such conflict-induced famine at the time.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
Regarding Wood's Dispatch, which of the following statements are true?
1. Grants-in-Aid system was introduced.
2. Establishment of universities was recommended.
3. English as a medium of instruction at all levels of education was recommended.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch of 1854 introduced the grants-in-aid system to support private schools and recommended setting up universities in the presidency towns, so statements 1 and 2 are correct. Statement 3 is wrong because it favoured the vernacular languages at the primary level, reserving English mainly for higher education.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: With reference to the Parliament of India, which of the following Parliamentary Committees scruitinizes and reports to the House whether the powers to make regulations, rules, sub-rules, by-laws, etc. conferred by the Constitution or delegated by the Parliament are being properly exercised by the Executive within the scope of such delegation?
questions.explanation: The Committee on Subordinate Legislation examines whether the executive is properly exercising the rule-making powers delegated to it by the Constitution or by Parliament, and reports to the House on this. The other committees deal with government assurances, procedural rules and scheduling of business.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. As per the Right to Education (RTE) Act, to be eligible for appointment as a teacher in a State, a person would be required to possess the minimum qualification laid down by the concerned State Council of Teacher Education.
2. As per the RTE Act, for teaching primary classes, a candidate is required to pass a Teacher Eligibility Test conducted in accordance with the National Council of Teacher Education guidelines.
3. In India, more than 90% of teacher education institutions are directly under the State Governments.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is wrong because minimum teacher qualifications under the RTE Act are laid down by the National Council for Teacher Education, not a State council. Statement 2 is correct, as candidates for primary teaching must clear a Teacher Eligibility Test framed on NCTE guidelines. The claim in statement 3 about institution ownership is not accurate.

questions.subject: Art & Culture
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Tradition State
1. ChapcharKut festival - Mizoram
2. KhongjomParba ballad - Manipur
3. Thang-Ta dance - Sikkim Which of the pairs given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Chapchar Kut is a well-known spring festival of Mizoram and Khongjom Parba is a ballad tradition of Manipur, so pairs 1 and 2 are correctly matched. Thang-Ta, a martial art form, belongs to Manipur rather than Sikkim, so pair 3 is wrong.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 replaced the Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954.
2. The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) is under the charge of Director General of Health Services in the Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 did consolidate and replace the older Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954, so statement 1 is correct. Statement 2 is wrong because the FSSAI is an autonomous body under the Health Ministry and is not placed under the charge of the Director General of Health Services.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: The term "two-state solution" is sometimes mentioned in the news in the context of the affairs of
questions.explanation: The phrase 'two-state solution' refers to the proposed settlement of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict through two separate states, Israel and a Palestinian state, existing side by side. It is therefore associated with Israel rather than the other countries listed.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to the provisions made under the National Food Security Act, 2013, consider the following statements:
1. The families coming under the category of 'below poverty line (BPL)' only are eligible to receive subsidies food grains.
2. The eldest woman in a household, of age 18 years or above, shall be the head of the household for the purpose of issuance of a ration card.
3. Pregnant women and lactating mothers are entitled to a 'take-home ration' of 1600 calories per day during pregnancy and for six months thereafter.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is wrong because the Act covers priority households up to a large share of the population, not just those below the poverty line. Statement 2 is correct, as the eldest woman aged 18 or above is treated as the head of household for the ration card. Statement 3 misstates the maternity entitlement, so it is incorrect.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: India enacted the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999 in order to comply with the obligations to
questions.explanation: India passed the Geographical Indications Act, 1999 to fulfil its obligations under the TRIPS agreement administered by the World Trade Organization. The ILO, IMF and UNCTAD are not concerned with the protection of geographical indications, so they are ruled out.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. In India, State Governments do not have the power to auction non- coal mines.
2. Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand do no have gold mines.
3. Rajasthan has iron ore mines.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Only statement 3 is correct, as Rajasthan does have iron ore deposits and mines. Statement 1 is wrong because State Governments are empowered to auction non-coal mineral blocks, and statement 2 is wrong because Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand do have gold-bearing areas.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to digital payments, consider the following statements:
1. BHIM app allows the user to tranfer money to anyone with a UPI-enabled bank account.
2. While a chip-pin debit card has four factors authentication, BHIM app has only two factors of authentication.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The BHIM app lets a user send money to anyone holding a UPI-enabled bank account, so statement 1 is correct. Statement 2 is not accurate in the way it contrasts the number of authentication factors of a chip-and-PIN card with the app, so it is treated as wrong.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text: Among the following cities, which one lies on a longitude closest to that of Delhi?
questions.explanation: Delhi lies at roughly 77 degrees East longitude, and among the options Bengaluru sits closest to this meridian. Hyderabad, Nagpur and Pune all lie noticeably further to the west, so their longitudes are farther from that of Delhi.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: International Labour Organization's Convention 138 and 182 are related to
questions.option_b: Adaptation of agricultural practices to global climate change
questions.option_c: Regulation of food prices and food security
questions.explanation: ILO Conventions 138 and 182 both address child labour: Convention 138 sets the minimum age for admission to employment, while Convention 182 targets the elimination of the worst forms of child labour. They are unrelated to agriculture, food prices or workplace gender parity.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: Regarding Money Bill, which of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_a: A bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it contains only provisions relating to imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax.
questions.option_b: A Money Bill has provisions for the custody of the Consolidated Fund of India or the Contingency Fund of India.
questions.option_c: A Money Bill is concerned with the appropriation of moneys out of the Contingency Fund of India.
questions.option_d: A Money Bill deals with the regulation of borrowing of money or giving of any guarantee by the Government of India.
questions.explanation: The statement that does not apply is the one on the Contingency Fund. A Money Bill deals with appropriation of moneys from the Consolidated Fund of India, not the Contingency Fund, so that description is incorrect. The other listed features, covering taxation, custody of funds and government borrowing, correctly reflect Article 110.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
With reference to the election of the President of India, consider the following statements:
1. The value of the vote of each MLA varies from State to State.
2. The value of the vote of MPs of the Lok Sabha is more than the value of the vote of MPs of the Rajya Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The value of an MLA's vote in the presidential election depends on the State's population and number of seats, so it varies from State to State, making statement 1 correct. Statement 2 is wrong because every Member of Parliament, whether from the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha, casts a vote of equal value.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: In the Indian context, what is the implication of ratifying the 'Additional Protocol' with the 'International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)'?
questions.option_a: The civilian nuclear reactors come under IAEA safeguards.
questions.option_b: The military nuclear installations come under the inspection of IAEA.
questions.option_c: The country will have the privilege to buy uranium from the Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG).
questions.option_d: The country automatically becomes a member of the NSG.
questions.explanation: Ratifying the Additional Protocol means India places its designated civilian nuclear reactors and facilities under IAEA safeguards and inspection. It does not extend inspection to military installations, nor does it by itself grant uranium purchase rights or NSG membership.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following countries:
1. Australia
2. Canada
3. China
4. India
5. Japan
6. USA
Which of the above are among the 'free-trade partners' of ASEAN?
questions.explanation: ASEAN's free-trade partners among those listed are Australia, China, India and Japan, which corresponds to the correct combination. Canada and the United States do not have such free-trade agreements with ASEAN, so options including them are wrong.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
With reference to the 'Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture (GACSA)', which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. GACSA is an outcome of the Climate Summit help in Paris in 2015.
2. Membership of GACSA does not create any binding obligations.
3. India was instrumental in the creation of GACSA.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Only statement 2 is correct: membership of the Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture is voluntary and creates no binding obligations. Statement 1 is wrong because GACSA emerged from the 2014 UN Climate Summit in New York, not the 2015 Paris meeting, and India was not among its promoters, making statement 3 wrong.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Which of the following is/are the aim/aims of "Digital India" Plan of the Government of India?
1. Formation of India's own Internet companies like China did.
2. Establish a policy framework to encourage overseas multinational corporations that collect Big Data to build their large data centers within our national geographical boundaries.
3. Connect many of our villages to the Internet and bring Wi-Fi to many of our schools, public places and major tourist centers.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Only statement 3 reflects a genuine aim of Digital India, which seeks to extend internet connectivity to villages and provide Wi-Fi in schools, public places and tourist centres. The programme does not aim to replicate China's model of home-grown internet firms or compel multinationals to build data centres in India.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Towns sometimes mentioned in news - Country
1. Aleppo - Syria
2. Kirkuk - Yemen
3. Mosul - Palestine
4. Mazar-i-sharif - Afghanistan Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Aleppo is a city in Syria and Mazar-i-Sharif lies in Afghanistan, so pairs 1 and 4 are correctly matched. Kirkuk and Mosul are both located in Iraq, not Yemen or Palestine, so those pairings are wrong.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: In the Federation established by The Government of India Act of 1935, residuary powers were given to the
questions.explanation: Under the federal scheme of the Government of India Act, 1935, the residuary powers of legislation were vested in the Governor General rather than any legislature. This distinguishes it from the later Indian Constitution, where residuary powers rest with the Union Parliament.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly shall vacate his/her office if he/she ceases to be a member of the assembly.
2. Whenever the Legislative Assembly is dissolved, the Speaker shall vacate his/her office immediately.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct because the Speaker of a Legislative Assembly must vacate office on ceasing to be a member of the House. Statement 2 is wrong, since even when the Assembly is dissolved the Speaker continues in office until immediately before the first sitting of the newly elected Assembly.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: Which one of the following reflects the most appropriate relationship between law and liberty?
questions.option_a: If there are more laws, there is less liberty.
questions.option_b: If there are no laws, there is no liberty.
questions.option_c: If there is liberty, laws have to be made by the people.
questions.option_d: If laws are changed too often, liberty is in danger.
questions.explanation: The most fitting view is that without laws there can be no liberty, because laws create the ordered framework within which individual freedoms are defined and protected. The claim that more laws always mean less liberty is too simplistic, since reasonable laws safeguard rather than diminish freedom.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. No criminal proceedings shall be instituted against the Governor of a State in any court during his term of office.
2. The emoluments and allowances of the Governor of a State shall not be diminished during his term of office.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Under the Constitution no criminal proceedings can be started against a Governor in any court while in office, and the emoluments and allowances of the Governor cannot be reduced during the term. These protections insulate the office from pressure.

questions.subject: Art & Culture
questions.text: The well-known painting "Bani Thani" belongs to the
questions.explanation: The celebrated painting 'Bani Thani', noted for its stylised female figure with elongated eyes and arched brows, belongs to the Kishangarh school of Rajasthani miniature painting. It is not associated with the Bundi, Jaipur or Kangra schools.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: What is "Terminal High Altitude Area Defence (THAAD)", sometimes seen in the news?
questions.option_d: A defence collaboration between Japan and South Korea
questions.explanation: THAAD stands for Terminal High Altitude Area Defence, an American anti-ballistic missile system designed to intercept short and medium-range missiles in their terminal phase. It is neither an Israeli radar, an Indian programme, nor a Japan-South Korea venture, so the other options are wrong.

questions.subject: Art & Culture
questions.text:
With reference to cultural history of India, consider the following statements:
1. Most of the Tyagaraja Kritis are devotional songs in praise of Lord Krishna.
2. Tyagaraja created several new ragas.
3. Annamacharya and Tyagaraja are contemporaries.
4. Annamacharyakirtanas are devotional songs in praise of Lord Venkateshwara.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 3 is wrong because Annamacharya lived well before Tyagaraja, so they were not contemporaries, and statement 1 is wrong because Tyagaraja's kritis mostly praise Lord Rama, not Krishna. Statements 2 and 4 are correct: Tyagaraja composed several new ragas, and Annamacharya's kirtanas honour Lord Venkateshwara.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text:
Which of the following are regarded as the main features of the "Rule of Law"?
1. Limitation of Powers
2. Equality before law
3. People's responsibility to the Government
4. Liberty and civil rights
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Limitation of governmental powers, equality before the law, and protection of liberty and civil rights are all core features of the Rule of Law, so statements 1, 2 and 4 are correct. The idea of people being responsible to the government inverts the concept, since the rule of law makes the government accountable to the law and the people.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the meaning of legal tender money?
questions.option_a: The money which is tendered in courts of law to defray the fee of legal cases
questions.option_b: The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement of his claims
questions.option_c: The bank money in the form of cheques, drafts, bills of exchange etc.
questions.option_d: The metallic money in circulation in a country
questions.explanation: Legal tender money is currency that a creditor is legally obliged to accept in discharge of a debt or claim. This distinguishes it from cheques and drafts, which no one is bound to accept, and from the narrower notion of only metallic coins in circulation.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: If a commodity is provided free to the public by the Government, then
questions.option_c: the opportunity costs is transferred from the consumers of the product to the tax-paying public.
questions.option_d: the opportunity cost is transferred from the consumers of the product to the Government.
questions.explanation: When a good is supplied free by the government, its production still uses scarce resources, so an opportunity cost exists but is met from public funds. The cost is effectively shifted from those consuming the good to the general body of taxpayers who finance it.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: Increase is absolute and per capital real GNP do not connote a higher level of economic development, if
questions.option_a: industrial output fails to keep pace with agricultural output.
questions.option_b: agricultural output fails to keep pace with industrial output.
questions.explanation: A rise in absolute and per capita real GNP need not signal genuine development if the gains bypass ordinary people, so that poverty and unemployment actually worsen. Development implies improvement in living standards and their distribution, not just growth in aggregate output.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
Human capital formation as a concept is better explained in terms of a process which enables 1. individuals of a country to accumulate more capital. 2. increasing the knowledge, skill levels and capacities of the people of the country. 3. accumulation of tangible wealth. 4. accumulation of intangible wealth.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Human capital formation is the process of raising people's knowledge, skills and capacities, which is a form of intangible wealth, so statements 2 and 4 are correct. It is not primarily about accumulating physical or tangible capital, which is why the options invoking tangible wealth are excluded.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text: Despite being a high saving economy, capital formation may not result in significant increase in output due to
questions.explanation: Even with a high savings rate, capital formation may add little to output when the capital-output ratio is high, meaning a large amount of investment is needed to produce each additional unit of output. This inefficiency in converting capital into production limits the growth in output.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text:
After the Santhal Uprising subsided, what was/were the measure/measures taken by the colonial government?
1. The territories called 'Santhal Paraganas' were created.
2. It became illegal for a Santhal to transfer land to a non-Santhal.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Both measures followed the suppression of the Santhal rebellion. The colonial government carved out the separate administrative unit of the Santhal Parganas, and it made it illegal for a Santhal to transfer land to a non-Santhal in order to protect the community.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: Economically, one of the results of the British rule in India in the 19th century was the
questions.option_a: increase in the export of Indian handicrafts
questions.option_b: growth in the number of Indian owned factories
questions.option_d: rapid inrease in the urban population
questions.explanation: A major economic effect of nineteenth-century British rule was the commercialisation of agriculture, as peasants increasingly grew cash crops for the market rather than for subsistence. Indian handicrafts declined rather than expanded, and there was no rapid rise in indigenous factories or urban population in this period.

questions.subject: Indian Polity
questions.text: If the President of India exercises his power as provided under Article 356 of the Constitution of a particular State, then
questions.option_a: the Assembly of the State is automatically dissolved.
questions.option_b: the powers of the Legislature of that State shall be exercisable by or under the authority of the Parliament.
questions.option_c: Article 19 is suspended in that State.
questions.option_d: the President can make laws relating to that State.
questions.explanation: When President's Rule is imposed under Article 356, the powers of the State legislature become exercisable by or under the authority of Parliament. The Assembly is not automatically dissolved, since it may only be suspended, and the provision neither suspends Article 19 by itself nor lets the President personally legislate.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Craft Heritage of
1. Puthukkuli shawls - Tamil Nadu
2. Sujni embroidery - Maharashtra
3. Uppada Jamdani saris - Karnataka Which ofthe pairs given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Only pair 1 is correct: Puthukkuli shawls are a craft associated with the Toda community of Tamil Nadu. Sujni embroidery belongs to Bihar rather than Maharashtra, and Uppada Jamdani saris come from Andhra Pradesh, not Karnataka, so those pairs are wrong.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.text:
In which of the following areas can GPS technology be used?
1. Mobile phone operations
2. Banking operations
3. Controlling the power grids
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation: GPS relies on precise satellite timing, which makes it useful well beyond navigation. It supports the synchronisation of mobile phone networks, time-stamping in banking transactions, and the coordinated operation of power grids, so all three applications are valid.

questions.subject: Indian Economy
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Reserve Bank of India manages and services Government of India Securities but not any State Government Securities.
2. Treasury bills are issued by the Government of India and there are no treasury bills issued by the state Governments.
3. Treasury bills offer are issued at a discount from the par value.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is wrong because the RBI manages and services the securities of State Governments as well as the Centre. Statement 2 is correct, as treasury bills are issued only by the Government of India, and statement 3 is correct, since they are sold at a discount to face value and redeemed at par.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Earth's magnetic field has reversed every few hundred thousand years.
2. When the Earth was created more than 4000 million years ago, there was 54% oxygen and no carbon dioxide.
3. When living organisms originated, they modified the early atmosphere of the Earth.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct, as the Earth's magnetic field has reversed polarity many times over geological history, and statement 3 is correct, since early life forms altered the atmosphere, notably by adding oxygen. Statement 2 is wrong because the early Earth's atmosphere did not contain 54 percent oxygen.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: The terms 'WannaCry, Petya and EternalBlue' sometimes mentioned in the news recently are related to
questions.explanation: WannaCry, Petya and EternalBlue are terms linked to cyber attacks. WannaCry and Petya were major ransomware outbreaks, while EternalBlue was the exploit they used to spread, so the context is cybersecurity rather than astronomy, currency or satellites.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text:
With reference to the circumstances in Indian agriculture, the concept of "Conservation Agriculture" assumes significance.
Which of the following fall under the Conservation Agriculture?
1. Avoiding the monoculture practices
2. Adopting minimum tillage
3. Avoiding the cultivation of plantation crops
4. Using crop residues to cover soil surface
5. Adopting spatial and temporal crop sequencing/crop rotations Select the correct answre using the code given below:
questions.explanation: Conservation agriculture rests on practices such as minimal soil disturbance through minimum tillage, keeping the soil covered with crop residues, and diversifying crops through rotations in space and time, so statements 2, 4 and 5 apply. It does not require avoiding plantation crops, so that option is excluded.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text: The term "sixth mass extinction/sixth extinction" is often mentioned in the news in the context of the discussion of
questions.option_a: Widespread monoculture practices in agriculture and large-scale commercial farming with indiscriminate use of chemicals in many parts of the world that may result in the loss of good native ecosystems.
questions.option_b: Fears of a possible collision of a meteorite with the Earth in the near future in the mannere it happened 65 million years ago that caused the mass extinction of many species including those of dinosaurs.
questions.option_c: Large scale cultivation of genetically modified crops in many parts of the world and promoting their cultivation in other parts of the world which may cause the disappearance of good native crop plants and the loss of food biodiversity.
questions.option_d: Mankind's over- exploitation/misuse of natural resources, framentation/loss of natural habitats, destruction of ecosystems, pollution and global climate change.
questions.explanation: The 'sixth mass extinction' refers to the ongoing, human-driven loss of species caused by overuse of natural resources, habitat fragmentation and destruction, pollution and climate change. It is a broad ecological concern rather than a single cause like monoculture, meteorite impact or genetically modified crops.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to the Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System (IRNSS), consider the following statements:
1. IRNSS has three satellites in geostationary and four satellites in geosynchronous orbits.
2. IRNSS covers entire India and about 5500 sq. km beyond its borders.
3. India will have its own satellite navigation system with full global coverage by the middle of 2019.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct: the IRNSS constellation comprises three satellites in geostationary and four in geosynchronous orbits. Statement 2 misstates the coverage beyond India's borders, and statement 3 is wrong because IRNSS is a regional system and was never intended to provide full global coverage.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Light is affected by gravity.
2. The Universe is constantly expanding.
3. Matter warps its surrounding space-time.
Which of the above is/are the prediction/predictions of Albert Einstein's General Theory of Relativity, often discussed in media?
questions.explanation: All three are consequences of Einstein's General Theory of Relativity. It predicts that light bends under gravity, that massive objects warp the surrounding space-time, and its equations are consistent with an expanding universe, so every statement is correct.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to the Genetically Modified mustard (GM mustard) developed in India, consider the following statements:
1. GM mustard has the genes of a soil bacterium that give the plant the property of pest-resistance to a wide variety of pests.
2. GM mustard has the genes that allow the plant cross- pollination and hybridization.
3. GM mustard has been developed jointly by the IARI and Punjab Agricultural University.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Only statement 2 is correct: GM mustard carries the barnase-barstar gene system that enables controlled cross-pollination and hybrid seed production. Statement 1 describes Bt-type pest resistance, which does not apply here, and statement 3 is wrong about the developing institutions, as it was developed by a Delhi University centre.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
Consider the following pairs:
Terms sometimes see in news Context/Topic
1. Belle II experiment - Artificial Intelligence
2. Blockchain technology - Digital/Cryptocurrency
3. CRISPR - Cas9 - Particle Physics Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Only pair 2 is correctly matched, since blockchain is the technology underlying digital and cryptocurrencies. The Belle II experiment relates to particle physics rather than artificial intelligence, and CRISPR-Cas9 is a gene-editing tool, not a topic in particle physics, so those pairs are wrong.

questions.subject: Environment & Ecology
questions.text: Which of the following statements best describes "carbon fertilization"?
questions.option_a: Increased plant growth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
questions.option_b: Increased temperature of Earth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
questions.option_c: Increased acidity of oceans as a result of increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
questions.option_d: Adaptation of all living beings on Earth to the climate change brought about by the increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
questions.explanation: Carbon fertilisation refers to the enhancement of plant growth that can occur when a higher concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere boosts photosynthesis. It is distinct from the warming, ocean acidification and general adaptation described in the other options.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.text: When the alarm of your smartphone rings in the morning, you wake up and tap it to stop the alarm which causes your geyser to be switched on automatically. The smart mirror in your bathroom shows the day's weather and also indicates the level of water in your overhead tank. After you take some groceries from your refrigerator for making breakfast, it recognises the shortage of stock in it and places an order for the supply of fresh grocery items. When you step out of your house and lock the door, all lights, fans, geysers and AC machines get switched off automatically. On your way to office, your car warns you about traffic congestion ahead and suggests an alternative route, and if you are late for a meeting, it sends a message to your office accordingly In the context of emerging communication technologies, which one of the following terms best applies to the above scenario?
questions.explanation: The scenario describes everyday devices, from alarms and geysers to refrigerators and cars, sensing conditions and communicating with one another over a network to act automatically. This interconnection of smart objects is the essence of the Internet of Things.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.text:
With reference to solar power production in India, consider the following statements:
1. India is the third largest in the world in the manufacture of silicon wafers used in photovoltaic units.
2. The solar power tariffs are determined by the Solar Energy Corporation of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are wrong. India is not the third largest manufacturer of the silicon wafers used in photovoltaic cells, and solar power tariffs are set through competitive bidding and regulatory processes rather than being determined by the Solar Energy Corporation of India.

questions.subject: Indian History
questions.text: The staple commodities of export by the English East Indian Company from Bengal in the middle of the 18th century were
questions.option_c: Copper, silver, gold, spices and tea
